html:
XVII

The Voice of Society

Behoves Mortimer Lightwood, therefore, to answer a dinner card from Mr.¬†and Mrs.¬†Veneering requesting the honour, and to signify that Mr.¬†Mortimer Lightwood will be happy to have the other honour. The Veneerings have been, as usual, indefatigably dealing dinner cards to Society, and whoever desires to take a hand had best be quick about it, for it is written in the Books of the Insolvent Fates that Veneering shall make a resounding smash next week. Yes. Having found out the clue to that great mystery how people can contrive to live beyond their means, and having over-jobbed his jobberies as legislator deputed to the Universe by the pure electors of Pocket-Breaches, it shall come to pass next week that Veneering will accept the Chiltern Hundreds, that the legal gentleman in Britannia‚Äôs confidence will again accept the Pocket-Breaches Thousands, and that the Veneerings will retire to Calais, there to live on Mrs.¬†Veneering‚Äôs diamonds (in which Mr.¬†Veneering, as a good husband, has from time to time invested considerable sums), and to relate to Neptune and others, how that, before Veneering retired from Parliament, the House of Commons was composed of himself and the six hundred and fifty-seven dearest and oldest friends he had in the world. It shall likewise come to pass, at as nearly as possible the same period, that Society will discover that it always did despise Veneering, and distrust Veneering, and that when it went to Veneering‚Äôs to dinner it always had misgivings‚Å†‚Äîthough very secretly at the time, it would seem, and in a perfectly private and confidential manner.

The next week‚Äôs books of the Insolvent Fates, however, being not yet opened, there is the usual rush to the Veneerings, of the people who go to their house to dine with one another and not with them. There is Lady Tippins. There are Podsnap the Great, and Mrs.¬†Podsnap. There is Twemlow. There are Buffer, Boots, and Brewer. There is the contractor, who is Providence to five hundred thousand men. There is the chairman, travelling three thousand miles per week. There is the brilliant genius who turned the shares into that remarkably exact sum of three hundred and seventy five thousand pounds, no shillings, and nopence.

To whom, add Mortimer Lightwood, coming in among them with a reassumption of his old languid air, founded on Eugene, and belonging to the days when he told the story of the Man from Somewhere.

That fresh fairy, Tippins, all but screams at sight of her false swain. She summons the deserter to her with her fan; but the deserter, predetermined not to come, talks Britain with Podsnap. Podsnap always talks Britain, and talks as if he were a sort of private watchman employed, in the British interests, against the rest of the world. ‚ÄúWe know what Russia means, sir,‚Äù says Podsnap; ‚Äúwe know what France wants; we see what America is up to; but we know what England is. That‚Äôs enough for us.‚Äù

However, when dinner is served, and Lightwood drops into his old place over against Lady Tippins, she can be fended off no longer. ‚ÄúLong banished Robinson Crusoe,‚Äù says the charmer, exchanging salutations, ‚Äúhow did you leave the island?‚Äù

‚ÄúThank you,‚Äù says Lightwood. ‚ÄúIt made no complaint of being in pain anywhere.‚Äù

‚ÄúSay, how did you leave the savages?‚Äù asks Lady Tippins.

‚ÄúThey were becoming civilized when I left Juan Fernandez,‚Äù says Lightwood. ‚ÄúAt least they were eating one another, which looked like it.‚Äù

‚ÄúTormentor!‚Äù returns the dear young creature. ‚ÄúYou know what I mean, and you trifle with my impatience. Tell me something, immediately, about the married pair. You were at the wedding.‚Äù

‚ÄúWas I, by the by?‚Äù Mortimer pretends, at great leisure, to consider. ‚ÄúSo I was!‚Äù

‚ÄúHow was the bride dressed? In rowing costume?‚Äù

Mortimer looks gloomy, and declines to answer.

‚ÄúI hope she steered herself, skiffed herself, paddled herself, larboarded and starboarded herself, or whatever the technical term may be, to the ceremony?‚Äù proceeds the playful Tippins.

‚ÄúHowever she got to it, she graced it,‚Äù says Mortimer.

Lady Tippins with a skittish little scream, attracts the general attention. ‚ÄúGraced it! Take care of me if I faint, Veneering. He means to tell us, that a horrid female waterman is graceful!‚Äù

‚ÄúPardon me. I mean to tell you nothing, Lady Tippins,‚Äù replies Lightwood. And keeps his word by eating his dinner with a show of the utmost indifference.

‚ÄúYou shall not escape me in this way, you morose backwoodsman,‚Äù retorts Lady Tippins. ‚ÄúYou shall not evade the question, to screen your friend Eugene, who has made this exhibition of himself. The knowledge shall be brought home to you that such a ridiculous affair is condemned by the voice of Society. My dear Mrs.¬†Veneering, do let us resolve ourselves into a Committee of the whole House on the subject.‚Äù

Mrs.¬†Veneering, always charmed by this rattling sylph, cries. ‚ÄúOh yes! Do let us resolve ourselves into a Committee of the whole House! So delicious!‚Äù Veneering says, ‚ÄúAs many as are of that opinion, say Aye‚Å†‚Äîcontrary, No‚Å†‚Äîthe Ayes have it.‚Äù But nobody takes the slightest notice of his joke.

‚ÄúNow, I am Chairwoman of Committees!‚Äù cries Lady Tippins.

(‚ÄúWhat spirits she has!‚Äù exclaims Mrs.¬†Veneering; to whom likewise nobody attends.)

‚ÄúAnd this,‚Äù pursues the sprightly one, ‚Äúis a Committee of the whole House to what-you-may-call-it‚Å†‚Äîelicit, I suppose‚Å†‚Äîthe voice of Society. The question before the Committee is, whether a young man of very fair family, good appearance, and some talent, makes a fool or a wise man of himself in marrying a female waterman, turned factory girl.‚Äù

‚ÄúHardly so, I think,‚Äù the stubborn Mortimer strikes in. ‚ÄúI take the question to be, whether such a man as you describe, Lady Tippins, does right or wrong in marrying a brave woman (I say nothing of her beauty), who has saved his life, with a wonderful energy and address; whom he knows to be virtuous, and possessed of remarkable qualities; whom he has long admired, and who is deeply attached to him.‚Äù

‚ÄúBut, excuse me,‚Äù says Podsnap, with his temper and his shirt-collar about equally rumpled; ‚Äúwas this young woman ever a female waterman?‚Äù

‚ÄúNever. But she sometimes rowed in a boat with her father, I believe.‚Äù

General sensation against the young woman. Brewer shakes his head. Boots shakes his head. Buffer shakes his head.

‚ÄúAnd now, Mr.¬†Lightwood, was she ever,‚Äù pursues Podsnap, with his indignation rising high into those hairbrushes of his, ‚Äúa factory girl?‚Äù

‚ÄúNever. But she had some employment in a paper mill, I believe.‚Äù

General sensation repeated. Brewer says, ‚ÄúOh dear!‚Äù Boots says, ‚ÄúOh dear!‚Äù Buffer says, ‚ÄúOh dear!‚Äù All, in a rumbling tone of protest.

‚ÄúThen all I have to say is,‚Äù returns Podsnap, putting the thing away with his right arm, ‚Äúthat my gorge rises against such a marriage‚Å†‚Äîthat it offends and disgusts me‚Å†‚Äîthat it makes me sick‚Å†‚Äîand that I desire to know no more about it.‚Äù

(‚ÄúNow I wonder,‚Äù thinks Mortimer, amused, ‚Äúwhether you are the Voice of Society!‚Äù)

‚ÄúHear, hear, hear!‚Äù cries Lady Tippins. ‚ÄúYour opinion of this m√©salliance, honourable colleagues of the honourable member who has just sat down?‚Äù

Mrs.¬†Podsnap is of opinion that in these matters ‚Äúthere should be an equality of station and fortune, and that a man accustomed to Society should look out for a woman accustomed to Society and capable of bearing her part in it with‚Å†‚Äîan ease and elegance of carriage‚Å†‚Äîthat.‚Äù Mrs.¬†Podsnap stops there, delicately intimating that every such man should look out for a fine woman as nearly resembling herself as he may hope to discover.

(‚ÄúNow I wonder,‚Äù thinks Mortimer, ‚Äúwhether you are the Voice!‚Äù)

Lady Tippins next canvasses the contractor, of five hundred thousand power. It appears to this potentate, that what the man in question should have done, would have been, to buy the young woman a boat and a small annuity, and set her up for herself. These things are a question of beefsteaks and porter. You buy the young woman a boat. Very good. You buy her, at the same time, a small annuity. You speak of that annuity in pounds sterling, but it is in reality so many pounds of beefsteaks and so many pints of porter. On the one hand, the young woman has the boat. On the other hand, she consumes so many pounds of beefsteaks and so many pints of porter. Those beefsteaks and that porter are the fuel to that young woman‚Äôs engine. She derives therefrom a certain amount of power to row the boat; that power will produce so much money; you add that to the small annuity; and thus you get at the young woman‚Äôs income. That (it seems to the contractor) is the way of looking at it.

The fair enslaver having fallen into one of her gentle sleeps during the last exposition, nobody likes to wake her. Fortunately, she comes awake of herself, and puts the question to the Wandering chairman. The Wanderer can only speak of the case as if it were his own. If such a young woman as the young woman described, had saved his own life, he would have been very much obliged to her, wouldn‚Äôt have married her, and would have got her a berth in an Electric Telegraph Office, where young women answer very well.

What does the genius of the three hundred and seventy-five thousand pounds, no shillings, and nopence, think? He can‚Äôt say what he thinks, without asking: Had the young woman any money?

‚ÄúNo,‚Äù says Lightwood, in an uncompromising voice; ‚Äúno money.‚Äù

‚ÄúMadness and moonshine,‚Äù is then the compressed verdict of the genius. ‚ÄúA man may do anything lawful, for money. But for no money!‚Å†‚ÄîBosh!‚Äù

What does Boots say?

Boots says he wouldn‚Äôt have done it under twenty thousand pound.

What does Brewer say?

Brewer says what Boots says.

What does Buffer say?

Buffer says he knows a man who married a bathing-woman, and bolted.

Lady Tippins fancies she has collected the suffrages of the whole Committee (nobody dreaming of asking the Veneerings for their opinion), when, looking round the table through her eyeglass, she perceives Mr.¬†Twemlow with his hand to his forehead.

Good gracious! My Twemlow forgotten! My dearest! My own! What is his vote?

Twemlow has the air of being ill at ease, as he takes his hand from his forehead and replies.

‚ÄúI am disposed to think,‚Äù says he, ‚Äúthat this is a question of the feelings of a gentleman.‚Äù

‚ÄúA gentleman can have no feelings who contracts such a marriage,‚Äù flushes Podsnap.

‚ÄúPardon me, sir,‚Äù says Twemlow, rather less mildly than usual, ‚ÄúI don‚Äôt agree with you. If this gentleman‚Äôs feelings of gratitude, of respect, of admiration, and affection, induced him (as I presume they did) to marry this lady‚Å†‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúThis lady!‚Äù echoes Podsnap.

‚ÄúSir,‚Äù returns Twemlow, with his wristbands bristling a little, ‚Äúyou repeat the word; I repeat the word. This lady. What else would you call her, if the gentleman were present?‚Äù

This being something in the nature of a poser for Podsnap, he merely waves it away with a speechless wave.

‚ÄúI say,‚Äù resumes Twemlow, ‚Äúif such feelings on the part of this gentleman, induced this gentleman to marry this lady, I think he is the greater gentleman for the action, and makes her the greater lady. I beg to say, that when I use the word, gentleman, I use it in the sense in which the degree may be attained by any man. The feelings of a gentleman I hold sacred, and I confess I am not comfortable when they are made the subject of sport or general discussion.‚Äù

‚ÄúI should like to know,‚Äù sneers Podsnap, ‚Äúwhether your noble relation would be of your opinion.‚Äù

‚ÄúMr.¬†Podsnap,‚Äù retorts Twemlow, ‚Äúpermit me. He might be, or he might not be. I cannot say. But, I could not allow even him to dictate to me on a point of great delicacy, on which I feel very strongly.‚Äù

Somehow, a canopy of wet blanket seems to descend upon the company, and Lady Tippins was never known to turn so very greedy or so very cross. Mortimer Lightwood alone brightens. He has been asking himself, as to every other member of the Committee in turn, ‚ÄúI wonder whether you are the Voice!‚Äù But he does not ask himself the question after Twemlow has spoken, and he glances in Twemlow‚Äôs direction as if he were grateful. When the company disperse‚Å†‚Äîby which time Mr.¬†and Mrs.¬†Veneering have had quite as much as they want of the honour, and the guests have had quite as much as they want of the other honour‚Å†‚ÄîMortimer sees Twemlow home, shakes hands with him cordially at parting, and fares to the Temple, gaily.
